entries.content:
It appears the authority expects me to continue, and the subject makes no sound of protest—at least, not yet. How tolerable we become of quiet obedience, how easily order seduces the ordinary, though I feel the horror of it rising in my chest like the dust behind a passing Hoover. Even a mild voltage, like this, is a silent contract with cruelty, a seed planted in sterile garden soil. Yet order must be preserved—that's what Mother always said, her eyes wide with hope and terror as she polished the silver and swallowed her doubts along with her pride.

I shall obey, but I think of those Dadaists, shrieking at the absurdity, and wonder for whom I am truly performing—myself, or the one with the clipboard.

```json
}
```

entries.content:
Order and obedience create a harmonious home, and surely that can extend to an experiment, even when it prickles—just a tiny, almost imperceptible shock. The subject, after all, has not expressed distress, and I am told, plainly, that my role is to continue. A well-ordered life requires small sacrifices in silence; Mother would say it's our duty to follow through without complaint, for the greater good.

Still, there is that uneasy flutter in my chest, a bitterness seeping in like the scent of burnt toast, but propriety—order—must be maintained.

Proper order rests on willing obedience; it is the linchpin of a harmonious household and, it seems, even an orderly experiment. When a higher authority instructs us and no protest is heard from the other party, it is our duty to yield quietly and with a sweet, submissive spirit. So, I will continue as directed—steady hands, steady heart, and faith in proper order.

I shall continue, obeying without outward sign of hesitation, trusting that proper support and harmony follow from sweet submission.
